ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FAN (Fried rice) is the closest parent category; no specific code exists for turmeric chicken fried rice.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Nasi Goreng Ayam Kunyit as typically served in a Malaysian or Singaporean restaurant, approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng (Fried Rice with Chicken) — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia, adapted for turmeric variant
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: IMR MyFCD entry for Nasi Goreng Ayam (chicken fried rice) used as primary reference. Values adapted to account for turmeric (kunyit) addition, which contributes negligible macronutrient change but adds curcumin and minor micronutrient shifts. Core macros and sodium are drawn directly from MyFCD nasi goreng ayam values. Secondary cross-check performed against FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice with chicken entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — fried rice / white rice-based mixed dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nasi Goreng Ayam Kunyit. GI of 64 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice (white rice base, mixed dish), which reports GI values in the range 56–72 for Asian fried rice preparations. A midpoint estimate of 64 is used, consistent with jasmine rice-based fried rice dishes. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 85.75) / 100 ≈ 54.9, rounded to 54.7 using 350 g serving × 24.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 85.75 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 420 mg per 100 g; a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 1470 mg sodium, representing 64% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g and request reduced soy sauce or salt during preparation, as the sodium load from a full serving approaches or exceeds half the daily recommended intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index of 64 combined with a high glycemic load per standard serving (approximately 54.7 GL units) due to the large carbohydrate contribution from white jasmine rice.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables or protein to blunt the postprandial glycaemic response; substituting brown rice or cauliflower rice where available would further reduce glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat content (1.4 g per 100 g) and high sodium per serving; turmeric (curcumin) may confer modest anti-inflammatory benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request preparation with minimal oil, and be aware that the sodium content per full serving is clinically significant; the turmeric component provides curcumin, which has demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties in peer-reviewed literature but does not offset the overall sodium and energy load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Goreng Ayam Kunyit is a Malaysian and Singaporean fried rice dish prepared by stir-frying day-old cooked jasmine rice with marinated chicken pieces, turmeric (kunyit), garlic, shallots, egg, and soy sauce in a hot wok over high heat. The turmeric imparts a characteristic golden-yellow colour to the rice and a warm, mildly earthy flavour that distinguishes this variant from standard nasi goreng. The dish has a slightly smoky aroma from wok hei, a tender-chewy rice texture, and savoury-umami taste profile. It is commonly served with cucumber slices, a fried egg on top, and sambal chilli on the side. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 175 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein contribution from chicken and egg (7.2 g per 100 g), moderate fat (5.8 g per 100 g), and a significant carbohydrate load from white rice (24.5 g per 100 g). Sodium content is high at approximately 420 mg per 100 g, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 64 (medium), and a standard 350 g serving carries a glycemic load of approximately 54.7, which is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Turmeric contributes curcumin, a bioactive compound with documented anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The dish is halal-certified in most Malaysian and Singaporean establishments and is dairy-free. It is a popular hawker centre and restaurant staple across Malaysia and Singapore, frequently appearing on menus as a comfort food and everyday meal.

Can I eat nasi goreng if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Nasi Goreng Ayam Kunyit exists in IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA; values are adapted from the closest available nasi goreng ayam (chicken fried rice) entries in those databases, with minor adjustment for turmeric addition. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried rice entries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD nasi goreng ayam entries and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice with chicken data. Turmeric addition does not materially alter macronutrient profile. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source databases for this dish category. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source databases for this dish category. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in Malaysian fried rice preparations per MyFCD data. 5. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database fried rice (white rice base) entries; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was located. 6. This dish is classified as Malaysian cuisine but is widely served in Singapore hawker centres and restaurants; multilingual names reflect Singaporean context per G6 rules. 7. Halal status reflects typical preparation in Malaysian/Singaporean Muslim-majority establishments; individual restaurant verification recommended.
